repository_conventions.summary: Page object methods perform actions and return locators. Assertions are written in the test body, not in page object methods.

findings.title: submitOrder() contains assertions that belong in the test body

evidence.summary: expect() calls added inside submitOrder() page object method

findings.why_it_matters: Assertions in page object methods couple the method to a specific expected outcome. If a test needs to verify a failed order submission, it cannot reuse submitOrder() without the assertion interfering. Keeping assertions in the test body keeps the page object reusable.
findings.suggested_fix: Remove the two expect() calls from submitOrder(). In the test body, add: `await expect(checkoutPage.page).toHaveURL('/order-confirmation');` after calling `await checkoutPage.submitOrder();`

findings.title: verifyItemInCart() is an assertion method on a page object

evidence.summary: New method verifyItemInCart() contains only expect() assertions — it is an assertion helper disguised as a page object method

findings.why_it_matters: The method name starts with 'verify' and contains only assertions. This is an assertion helper, not a page action. Placing it on the page object mixes concerns and makes the test body opaque — callers cannot see what is being asserted without reading the page object.
findings.suggested_fix: Remove verifyItemInCart() from the page object. Add a locator getter instead: `cartItem(productName: string) { return this.page.getByTestId('cart-item').filter({ hasText: productName }); }` and write the assertions in the test body.

top_risks: pages/CheckoutPage.ts: assertion methods on the page object couple it to specific expected outcomes, reducing reusability
